cards.title: Men's Health — Overview & Hormonal Framework
cards._legacyHtml:
Men's Health in Naturopathic Practice
Men's health encompasses hormonal, reproductive, cardiovascular, metabolic, and psychological wellbeing. Men are statistically less likely to seek healthcare — shorter consultations, later presentations, higher rates of undiagnosed conditions. Naturopathic practice can bridge this gap through holistic, non-judgemental assessment addressing physical and psychological dimensions of male health.
HormoneNormal Range (Adult Male)Key FunctionsSigns of Deficiency
Total Testosterone10–35 nmol/L (300–1000 ng/dL)Libido, muscle mass, bone density, mood, erythropoiesisFatigue, low libido, ED, depression, reduced muscle, osteoporosis
Free Testosterone0.2–0.6 nmol/LBiologically active fraction — only ~2% of totalSymptoms of low T despite "normal" total T if SHBG elevated
SHBG10–57 nmol/LBinds testosterone — reduces bioavailabilityHigh SHBG: obesity, liver disease, ageing → functional hypogonadism
LH2–9 IU/LStimulates Leydig cells → testosterone productionLow LH + low T = secondary hypogonadism (pituitary/hypothalamic)
FSH1.5–12 IU/LStimulates Sertoli cells → spermatogenesisLow FSH = impaired spermatogenesis. High FSH = primary testicular failure
Oestradiol (E2)40–160 pmol/L (11–44 pg/mL)Bone health, libido, cardiovascular. Converted from T by aromataseHigh E2 in obesity (excess aromatase in adipose) → gynaecomastia, low libido, infertility
DHT1.0–3.0 nmol/LMost potent androgen — virilisation, libido, prostate growth, hair lossElevated DHT: BPH, androgenic alopecia, acne. Low DHT: impaired sexual development
Prolactin<400 mIU/LInhibits GnRH → reduces LH/FSH → reduces testosteroneHyperprolactinaemia: low T, ED, gynaecomastia, infertility — rule out prolactinoma

cards.title: Testosterone Deficiency — Naturopathic Assessment & Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Late-Onset Hypogonadism (LOH)
Testosterone declines approximately 1–2% per year after age 30. By age 70, ~30% of men have clinically low testosterone. LOH is underdiagnosed because symptoms are non-specific and often attributed to "normal ageing." Symptoms + low morning total T (<10.4 nmol/L) on 2 occasions = biochemical confirmation. Always test in the morning (peak testosterone).
1
Symptom Assessment — ADAM Questionnaire Androgen Deficiency in Ageing Males: (1) Decreased libido, (2) Lack of energy, (3) Decreased strength/endurance, (4) Lost height, (5) Decreased enjoyment of life, (6) Sadness/grumpy, (7) Erections less strong, (8) Deterioration in sport, (9) Falling asleep after dinner, (10) Decreased work performance. Score ≥1 (Q1 or Q7) OR ≥3 others = ADAM positive — investigate.
2
Naturopathic Testosterone Support Protocol Lifestyle foundations: weight loss (visceral fat → aromatase → ↑ oestrogen, ↓ T). Sleep optimisation (70% of testosterone secreted during sleep — <5hrs sleep = 15% T reduction). Resistance training (compound movements 3×/week — acute T spike + long-term androgen receptor upregulation). Reduce alcohol (>14 units/week suppresses T by 30%). Chronic stress reduction (cortisol antagonises testosterone at receptor level).
3
Nutritional Support Zinc 15–30mg/day (rate-limiting cofactor for testosterone synthesis — most common nutritional deficiency in low T). Vitamin D 2000–4000 IU/day (VDR in Leydig cells — Vit D deficiency strongly correlated with low T). Ashwagandha KSM-66 600mg/day ★★★ (RCT: +15–18% T, improved sperm quality, reduced cortisol). Fenugreek seed extract 600mg/day (inhibits SHBG binding, ↑ free T). Magnesium 400mg/day (reduces SHBG). D-Aspartic acid 3g/day (stimulates LH release — short-term use). Avoid: soy isoflavones in excess, lavender/tea tree oil (topical oestrogenic effects).
SupplementDoseEvidenceMechanism
Ashwagandha (KSM-66)300–600mg BD★★★ Multiple RCTs↓ Cortisol → ↑ T. ↑ LH. Antioxidant effect on sperm
Zinc15–30mg/day elemental★★★ Well establishedRequired for testosterone synthesis (5α-reductase cofactor), LH receptor sensitivity
Vitamin D32000–4000 IU/day★★★ RCT: +25% T in deficient menVDR in Leydig cells — direct steroidogenic action
Fenugreek500–600mg/day★★ Multiple RCTsInhibits SHBG + aromatase → ↑ free T + ↓ E2
Tongkat Ali (Eurycoma)200–400mg/day★★ RCT evidence growing↑ Free T by reducing SHBG binding. Adaptogen effects

cards.title: Erectile Dysfunction — Integrative Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
ED as a Vascular Marker
Erectile dysfunction (ED) is often the first manifestation of systemic vascular disease — penile arteries (1–2mm) develop atherosclerosis before coronary arteries (3–4mm). ED in a man under 50 is a cardiovascular risk marker requiring full CV assessment. MMAS: men with ED have 1.5× increased MI risk. Always screen for CV risk factors in ED.
ED TypeMechanismKey FeaturesPrimary Approach
Vasculogenic (most common ~70%)Endothelial dysfunction → ↓ NO → ↓ smooth muscle relaxationGradual onset, absent morning erections, CV risk factorsCardiovascular risk reduction, L-arginine, exercise, diet
NeurogenicImpaired autonomic innervationPost-prostatectomy, diabetes, MS, spinal cord injuryAlpha-lipoic acid, B12, acupuncture, pelvic floor PT
HormonalLow testosterone, hyperprolactinaemia, hypothyroidismLow libido + ED + fatigueAddress underlying hormonal cause first
PsychogenicPerformance anxiety, depression, relationship issuesSituational (OK with one partner, not another), sudden onset, morning erections preservedCBT, sex therapy, mindfulness, stress reduction
Mixed (most common real-world)Combination of aboveGradual onset with psychological overlayAddress all contributing factors simultaneously
1
Naturopathic ED Protocol L-arginine 3–6g/day (NO precursor — endothelial vasodilation. Enhanced by Pycnogenol 80–120mg/day ★★★ — combination RCT showed 92% restoration of normal sexual function). Ginseng (Panax) 900mg TID ★★ (IIEF score improvement). Exercise: 30–40 min aerobic 4×/week = significant ED improvement equivalent to PDE5 inhibitor in mild-moderate ED. Mediterranean diet adherence correlates inversely with ED severity. Pelvic floor exercises — strengthen ischiocavernosus and bulbocavernosus muscles.

cards.title: Prostate Health — BPH & Prevention
cards._legacyHtml:
Prostate in Ageing
BPH (Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia) affects 50% of men by age 60 and 90% by age 85. Prostate cancer is the most common male cancer in the West. BNYS practitioners can support prostate health through diet, lifestyle, and evidence-based phytotherapy — while ensuring appropriate medical screening (PSA + DRE) and timely referral.
ConditionKey FeatureNaturopathic ApproachEvidence
BPHLUTS (frequency, nocturia, weak stream, incomplete emptying)Saw palmetto 320mg/day, pumpkin seed oil 1g TID, stinging nettle root 300mg BD, lycopene 15–30mg/day, zinc 30mg/day, beta-sitosterol★★★ Saw palmetto comparable to finasteride in mild-moderate BPH (Cochrane)
Prostate Cancer PreventionPSA screening + DRE — annual after 50 (or 40 if family history)Lycopene ≥15mg/day (tomatoes, watermelon), selenium 200mcg/day, green tea EGCG 400mg/day, curcumin, cruciferous vegetables (sulforaphane → NRF2)★★ Epidemiological + mechanistic evidence. No RCT for primary prevention
Prostatitis (Chronic/CPPS)Pelvic pain, urinary symptoms, without infectionPollen extract (Cernilton) 126mg TID ★★, quercetin 500mg BD ★★, omega-3, acupuncture, pelvic floor physiotherapy, stress reduction (HPA axis contributes)★★ Quercetin RCT: significant pain + QoL improvement
🟡 PSA — When to Refer
PSA >4 ng/mL = refer to urology. PSA velocity >0.75 ng/mL/year (rate of rise) = suspicious regardless of absolute level. PSA density >0.15 = suspicious. Free:total PSA ratio <15% = more likely malignant (total PSA bound to protein). DRE: hard, nodular, irregular prostate = refer urgently. ALWAYS refer: PSA >10, suspicious DRE, haematuria + LUTS, bone pain in older man (metastatic prostate cancer).

cards.title: Male Fertility & Sperm Health
cards._legacyHtml:
Male Factor Infertility
Male factor contributes to 40–50% of infertility cases. WHO 2021 normal semen parameters: Volume ≥1.4ml, Total sperm ≥39 million per ejaculate, Concentration ≥16 million/ml, Total motility ≥42%, Progressive motility ≥30%, Morphology ≥4% normal (Kruger strict criteria).
ParameterNaturopathic InterventionEvidence
Sperm count (oligospermia)Ashwagandha 300mg BD ★★★, CoQ10 200–300mg/day ★★★, zinc 30mg, selenium 200mcg, folic acid 5mg★★★ CoQ10 meta-analysis: +14% sperm count, +10% motility
Motility (asthenospermia)L-carnitine 2–3g/day ★★★, CoQ10, vitamin C 1g/day, antioxidant combination protocol★★★ L-carnitine: significant motility improvement in multiple RCTs
Morphology (teratospermia)Lycopene 4–8mg/day, selenium 200mcg, antioxidant protocol, heat avoidance (laptops, hot baths, tight underwear)★★ Lycopene: +22% improvement in morphology (RCT)
DNA fragmentationNAC 600mg BD, vitamin C + E, astaxanthin 16mg/day, smoking cessation, alcohol reduction★★ Oxidative stress is primary cause — antioxidants reduce DFI significantly
